Selected work

Case studies

Case study 01

Message Capping NUX

Meta · WhatsApp Business · Shipped March 2026

$1B+ product line

Content leadership Cross-functional Monetization NUX

Problem

WhatsApp was introducing message caps for consumers to drive WhatsApp Business adoption. Four teams disagreed on how to frame it, but the real issue wasn't the copy. It was a framing problem. Each team had a different answer for what this moment was: a product launch, a policy enforcement, a consumer protection measure, or a business transparency signal. The copy reflected that confusion.

What I did

Before suggesting updates, I had to establish what this moment actually was. I pointed out that we weren't announcing a feature. We were setting a standard. That shift changed everything. The early consumer-side title read "New ways to limit message overload," and the business-side draft said the limit applied to people who "never messaged you." One sounded like a feature launch. The other read as an accusation. Neither was neutral. I rewrote the copy rationale based on what changed, why, and what a business could do about it. The cap was set high enough that accounts hitting it were likely spammers, not legitimate businesses. The path for legitimate businesses was also made clear. When GTM pushed back, worried that naming the cap directly might create consumer backlash, I proposed a third path: a standalone half-sheet with a toast notification. No editorial framing, no value prop, just the facts. "There's now a monthly limit on new chats" is the headline both sides landed on, replacing "new ways" and "never messaged you" altogether. GTM and Product accepted, resolving 53 review comments without escalation.

Outcome

My informational positioning became GTM's standard going forward. Capping contributed to Business Messaging Monetization's 170% incremental revenue increase and 7x monthly revenue lift.

+170%

Case study 02

Dynamic Audiences Naming

Meta · WhatsApp Business · Shipped April 2026

$1B+ product line

Naming systems Information architecture 74 countries

Problem

SMB users navigated jargon like "Set as dynamic audience," "List name," and table-formatted descriptions just to reach their own customers. The existing labels were inherited from database architecture, which PM and Engineering were accustomed to. A naming standard didn't exist.

What I did

I pushed for a naming standard and made the case that jargony labels were creating real friction for actual business owners. My process started with the user journey: don't force users to decode names for suggested audiences. I mapped suggested audience names with logical descriptions, considering what each audience represented and how a name reinforced it. I landed on "Active chats," "Inactive chats," and "Your WhatsApp contacts" to name collections a small business owner could recognize. I also named the "Automatic updates" toggle and wrote snackbar copy for both states. I flagged that the active/inactive split might be confusing because both lists used a 30-day cutoff, meaning the same chat could appear in both. Rather than treating that as a copy problem, I escalated it as a product logic bug for engineering to fix. Research validated the label framework with real users before we finalized. Naming isn't decoration, because when you get it right, users stop noticing the interface and start accomplishing their goals.

Outcome

Naming became the standard across all audience surfaces and shipped to 74 countries. Users were getting to their goal faster because the language made sense to them.

4.3×
Picker-to-Composer conversion

Case study 03

GenAI Content Strategy

Meta · WhatsApp Business · Shipped May 2026

GenAI Image Composer

GenAI Content strategy Terminology NUX

Problem

WhatsApp Business was integrating GenAI, and technical vocabulary had been baked into the entire surface from the start. The word "generate" implies the AI does all the work and always delivers something perfect. The first time a user generates an undesirable image, they feel like the product failed, when it's really a tool they're learning to use. That's a trust problem, not just a word problem.

What I did

I started with a question: what expectation does "generate" set, and does the product actually meet it? From there I mapped the full content journey: every label, CTA, screen name, and confirmation message needed to feel like part of a coherent pattern. I chose "create" because it puts the user in the driver's seat, implying collaboration and iteration, not a takeover. I named the screen "Create AI images" and resolved ambiguous CTAs, moving from "Select how you want to generate AI images: From a message or From a photo" to "Start by using a message, photo, or description: Use a message or Use a photo."

Outcome

The "create" terminology was adopted as the standard across GenAI surfaces. The content strategy question here wasn't new. It's the same one I've always worked on: how do you help users understand what a product can and can't do, so they trust it enough to keep using it?

+22.8%
Messages delivered

Advisor-client trading UI

Charles Schwab

Error message rewrite

Simplified an overly apologetic, multi-line 404 into two words and one sentence. Shifted ownership: "Can't find page" (our issue) became "Page not found" (accurate framing).

Reduced cognitive load

String review at scale

Set a goal of 750 strings reviewed in 3 months. Built a scalable TextID-based workflow and reviewed 3,500+ strings, 4× the target, without changing underlying code.

3,500+ strings · scalable process

Content Design & Strategy roles

Over 15 years of experience in UX writing, content strategy, and information architecture across enterprise, consumer, and fintech products.

I'm a Senior Content Designer and UX Writer with a career spanning Meta, Apple, Google, Schwab, and beyond. I specialize in high-stakes, regulated, and monetization-sensitive environments where the words have to earn their place.

My work lives at the intersection of clarity, systems thinking, and user trust. I believe the best content decisions come from understanding the whole product, not just the string.

When I'm not writing, I'm at Stinson Beach, walking my beagle Princess Leia, or sharpening my storytelling at Toastmasters.

Let's work together.
